01a_quotes.title: Combining Human Expertise with Artificial Intelligence: Experimental Evidence from Radiology

study_design: We design and run an experiment with professional radiologists and develop an empirical methodology that aims to answer these questions.

Our experimental design compares human and AI performance, quantifies the predictive value of the information that humans hold but AI tools do not—henceforth termed contextual information—and tests whether AI assistance improves human performance.
Our treatments vary the information set radiologists have access to when making decisions, using a two-by-two factorial design.
We address these challenges using a combination of three different experimental designs, each with certain advantages and disadvantages.
In the first design, we assign participants to a random sequence of the four information treatments. Each information condition uses a different set of fifteen patients at random without repetition.

In this design, radiologists diagnose each patient in each of the four information environments. This design will allow us to estimate an empirical analog to equation (4) and compare it with the participant's report with AI assistance.

In the third design, we address residual concerns that our participants may nonetheless remember the AI prediction from a previous round. Participants are asked to diagnose fifty patients, first without and then with AI assistance.
The experiment was pre-registered on the AEA registry, number AEARCTR-0009620.
task: The experiment includes 227 professional radiologists recruited through teleradiology companies to diagnose retrospective patient cases.

Radiologists diagnose the presence of a given pathology at the request of a treating physician. They rely on information from diagnostic images (e.g., chest X-rays), relevant clinical history (e.g., laboratory results), and clinical indication notes of the treating physician.

We elicit participants' beliefs regarding the probability of the presence of a pathology ph (ωi = 1|s) and a recommended treatment/follow-up decision aih under information treatments that vary the signals.
A radiologists belief that a pathology is present given the available information, i.e. ph (ω = 1|s), is elicited using a continuous slider.
We also collect a binary "treatment/follow-up" recommendation for each pathology that is not definitively ruled out, which we interpret as a∗h (s).
In addition to ph (ω = 1|s) and a∗h (s), we record active time and clickstream data that result from the interaction with the interface.
population: The experiment includes 227 professional radiologists recruited through teleradiology companies to diagnose retrospective patient cases.
Approximately 14% of our participants are US-based, 15% have a degree from a US institution, 44% are affiliated with a large clinic, and 63% with an academic institution.
Approximately 60% of the participants had previous experience working with AI tools for radiology, and all participants routinely diagnose US patients.

Participants for the first and third designs, which constitute the majority, were recruited through teleradiology companies that serve US healthcare providers and hire both US-based and foreign radiologists.

The second design required us to work with a partner who could guarantee subjects' participation over several months. We collaborated with VinMec healthcare system in Vietnam to recruit their staff radiologists.
In total, 227 radiologists participated in our experiment.
arms: In the minimal information environment, we provide only the chest X-ray image to which we add either AI predictions, contextual information, or both.
This results in four possible information conditions: X-ray only (XO); clinical history without AI (CH); AI without clinical history (AI); and both clinical history and AI (AI+CH).
The AI information treatment provides probabilities that a patient is positive for potential chest pathologies.

The contextual information treatment provides clinical history information that radiologists typically have available but, for logistic and legal data privacy reasons, is difficult to obtain to train the AI. This information includes the treating doctors' indications, the patient's vitals, and the patient's labs.

Using the algorithm in Irvin et al. (2019), which was trained on approximately 250,000 X-rays with corresponding disease labels, the AI information treatment provides probabilities that a patient is positive for potential chest pathologies.
outcome_definitions: We will evaluate the quality of assessments by both AI and our participants against a diagnostic standard for each patient-pathology.

We construct a diagnostic standard by aggregating the assessments of five board-certified radiologists practicing at a highly reputed hospital with at least ten years of experience and chest radiology as a sub-specialty.
We classify ωi = 1 if ∑h πh (ωi = 1|sH i,h)/5 > 0.5.
Radiologists correctly recommend treatment in 70.4% of patient-pathology pairs.
A unit of observation is a radiologist decision (or prediction) for a given patient and pathology (indexed by i).
Deviation from diagnostic standard is the absolute difference between the reported probability and the diagnostic standard.
Deviation from AI is the absolute difference between the human's reported probability and the AI's reported probability.
Correct decision is an indicator for whether the decision matches the diagnostic standard.
Active time is measured in seconds.
statistical_results: Radiologists correctly recommend treatment in 70.4% of patient-pathology pairs.
On average, they spend 2.79 minutes per patient with large variability.

For instance, the average deviation from the diagnostic standard, which is defined as |ph (ωi = 1|siht) − ωi|, for the three designs ranges from 0.21 to 0.23, and average active time ranges from 155 to 173 seconds.
The average deviation from the diagnostic standard, which is defined as |ph (ωi = 1|siht) − ωi|, for the three designs ranges from 0.21 to 0.23.
Average active time ranges from 155 to 173 seconds.
AI with an AUC of 0.85 is more accurate than 78% of humans.
A hypothetical decision maker that optimally combines both signals has an AUC of 0.88 and would be more accurate than 86% of all humans in the sample.
Access to contextual information improves performance on average. We find that access to clinical history reduces the deviation from the diagnostic standard by 4.0% (p < 0.05) of the control mean.
In contrast, AI assistance does not significantly improve average performance.
Treatments where AI is provided reduce this baseline average deviation by 18.3%.
We find that in the model that best describes the data, agents exhibit automation neglect and signal dependence neglect.
The first type of deviation occurs when agents do not put the correct relative weight on the AI information.
We say that an agent exhibits automation bias (see Alberdi et al., 2009) if they over-weight the AI information relative to their own and automation neglect if they under-weight it.

We find that in the model that best describes the data, agents exhibit automation neglect and signal dependence neglect. Although parsimonious, we find that this model replicates the empirical patterns observed in the data.
Automation bias (b) 0.26 (0.02), Own information bias (d) 0.87 (0.01)
The median cost of a false positive across both top-level pathologies with AI assistance is one half the cost of a false negative.
For both ω = 0 and ω = 1 reported probabilities are upwards-sloping in the AI prediction, which means that human and AI signal are not conditionally independent given ω.

The results reveal that AI assistance improves performance on both metrics when radiologists express uncertainty, but harms performance when radiologists are highly confident that a pathology is absent.
When the AI provides a confident prediction that a pathology is absent (i.e. close to zero), performance improves significantly.
In the lowest bin of AI predictions, deviation from the diagnostic standard markedly decreases.
In the second highest bin we also see a performance improvement, although this effect is not statistically significant because the sample size is small.

However, in the middle range of signals, where the confidence of the AI is low (meaning the AI signal is not close to either zero or one), radiologists' diagnostic performance and probability of making a correct decision are lower when AI information is provided.

The results of this exercise mirror the treatment effects: because radiologists do not correctly incorporate the AI's information, the majority of patients are optimally decided either by the radiologist or the AI alone but not by the radiologist with access to AI.
For both a Bayesian and the observed behavior in our experiment, we find that the AI decides almost all cases if the cost of a false negative is less than $100 per case.

For Bayesians, the share of cases that involve human-AI collaboration rises markedly above a cost of $100, but even for costs as high as $10,000, 21% of cases are delegated to the AI to save on human effort because the Bayesian and AI decisions coincide.
When we conduct the same exercise and use the observed behavior of human radiologists, we find that humans are involved in 50% of cases if the cost of a false negative is sufficiently large.
Moreover, the majority of cases where a human is involved have the human make decisions without AI assistance.
An unassisted radiologist (H) takes 2.8 minutes per patient, and incurs a relative decision loss of approximately 12.4.
By moving to the frontier point that minimizes decision loss, one can reduce decision loss while also saving $5.0 in time costs.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Although this standard may not perfectly capture a "ground truth," patients would likely benefit from a system that brings diagnoses closer to the aggregate opinion of several highly qualified and experienced experts.

Unfortunately, medical records are of limited value because definitive diagnostic tests do not exist for most thoracic pathologies and, even when they do exist, are selectively performed depending on a radiologist's recommendation.
Due to the high cost of recruiting radiologists at market rates, an across-participant design is impractical to power, except for very large effect sizes.

To ensure that our results do not rely on this "wash-out" being successful, a third design obtains an assessment with AI assistance only after assessments without AI assistance have been obtained. However, this third design is subject to order effects.
We find no evidence of order effects on diagnostic quality although there is evidence that familiarity with the interface increases the speed with which participants diagnose patients.
This approach is best suited to contexts in which the focal pathology of interest for a patient is clear to a treating physician.

The second caveat is that collaborative systems may change humans' expectations about the difficulty of cases (see Agarwal et al., 2025) or cause them to strategically respond. We leave such extensions to future work.

The biases we discover and the unrealized potential gains of AI assistance motivate further study of the potential benefits from and type of training or experience that could improve the performance of human-AI collaboration.

While our findings have important implications for the large class of statistical decision problems, it is an open question whether they translate to other types of AI assistance. For instance, our observation that humans optimally work next to as opposed to with AI may not necessarily hold for other cases such as generative AI.

Although we find that human radiologists and AI are substitutes on specific tasks (as defined by diagnosis of particular cases), they may still be complements in the overall production function which involves a bundle of different tasks (Acemoglu and Autor, 2011).

01a_quotes.notes: This is an experimental RCT with 227 professional radiologists using a 2x2 factorial design (AI × Clinical History) with three different experimental designs to address methodological challenges. The paper combines treatment effect analysis with structural estimation of a model of belief updating (Grether 1980/1992 framework) and optimal delegation analysis. Key finding: AI assistance does not improve average radiologist performance despite AI being more accurate than 78% of radiologists, due to systematic biases in how radiologists incorporate AI information (automation neglect and signal dependence neglect). The optimal delegation policy suggests cases should be decided by either humans or AI alone, rarely by humans with AI assistance.

01_extraction.title: Combining Human Expertise with Artificial Intelligence: Experimental Evidence from Radiology
01_extraction.study_design_description: An experiment with 227 professional radiologists using a two-by-two factorial design that varies the information set available when making diagnostic decisions. The experiment compares human and AI performance, quantifies the predictive value of contextual information, and tests whether AI assistance improves human performance. Three different experimental designs were employed: the first assigns participants to a random sequence of four information treatments with fifteen patients each; the second has radiologists diagnose patients in each of four information environments; the third addresses concerns about memory effects by having participants diagnose fifty patients first without and then with AI assistance.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Professional radiologists diagnose the presence of a given pathology in retrospective patient cases by relying on diagnostic images (e.g., chest X-rays), relevant clinical history (e.g., laboratory results), and clinical indication notes. Participants elicit their beliefs regarding the probability of the presence of a pathology using a continuous slider and provide a binary treatment/follow-up recommendation for each pathology not definitively ruled out. Active time and clickstream data are also recorded.
01_extraction.population_description: 227 professional radiologists recruited through teleradiology companies to diagnose retrospective patient cases. Approximately 14% are US-based, 15% have a degree from a US institution, 44% are affiliated with a large clinic, and 63% with an academic institution. Approximately 60% had previous experience working with AI tools for radiology, and all participants routinely diagnose US patients. The majority were recruited through teleradiology companies serving US healthcare providers; a subset was recruited from VinMec healthcare system in Vietnam.

arms.description: X-ray only (XO): minimal information environment providing only the chest X-ray image
Clinical history without AI (CH): provides clinical history information including treating doctors' indications, patient's vitals, and patient's labs
AI without clinical history (AI): provides probabilities that a patient is positive for potential chest pathologies, trained on approximately 250,000 X-rays
Both clinical history and AI (AI+CH): provides both AI predictions and contextual information

outcomes.outcome_description: An indicator for whether the decision matches the diagnostic standard, which is constructed by aggregating assessments of five board-certified radiologists with at least ten years of experience
The absolute difference between the reported probability and the diagnostic standard, defined as |ph(ωi = 1|siht) − ωi|
Time spent by radiologist on diagnosis task
The absolute difference between the human's reported probability and the AI's reported probability

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Radiologists correctly recommend treatment in 70.4% of patient-pathology pairs.
On average, they spend 2.79 minutes per patient with large variability.
The average deviation from the diagnostic standard, which is defined as |ph(ωi = 1|siht) − ωi|, for the three designs ranges from 0.21 to 0.23
Average active time ranges from 155 to 173 seconds.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: AI more accurate than 78% of humans
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: AI with an AUC of 0.85 is more accurate than 78% of humans.

reported_statistics.effect_in_outcome_units: optimal combination more accurate than 86% of humans
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: A hypothetical decision maker that optimally combines both signals has an AUC of 0.88 and would be more accurate than 86% of all humans in the sample.
Access to contextual information improves performance on average. We find that access to clinical history reduces the deviation from the diagnostic standard by 4.0% (p < 0.05) of the control mean.
Treatments where AI is provided reduce this baseline average deviation by 18.3%.
In contrast, AI assistance does not significantly improve average performance.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: structural model - own information bias parameter
ai_only vs xray_only - low AI confidence range
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: When the AI provides a confident prediction that a pathology is absent (i.e. close to zero), performance improves significantly. In the lowest bin of AI predictions, deviation from the diagnostic standard markedly decreases.

reported_statistics.arm_comparison: ai_only vs xray_only - high radiologist confidence
reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: The results reveal that AI assistance improves performance on both metrics when radiologists express uncertainty, but harms performance when radiologists are highly confident that a pathology is absent.
An unassisted radiologist (H) takes 2.8 minutes per patient, and incurs a relative decision loss of approximately 12.4.

01_extraction.notes: Study pre-registered on AEA registry (AEARCTR-0009620). Total sample size is 227 radiologists; arm-level sample sizes not explicitly stated in quotes. The paper uses a 2×2 factorial design with four information conditions (XO, CH, AI, AI+CH) and three different experimental designs to address methodological challenges including potential memory effects and order effects. Key finding: despite AI being more accurate than 78% of radiologists (AUC 0.85), AI assistance does not significantly improve average radiologist performance due to systematic biases in information incorporation—specifically automation neglect (under-weighting AI) and signal dependence neglect. The paper reports structural parameter estimates from a belief-updating model (Grether framework) showing automation bias coefficient of 0.26 (SE 0.02) and own information bias of 0.87 (SE 0.01). Diagnostic standard constructed from five board-certified radiologists with ≥10 years experience. No perfect ground truth available for most thoracic pathologies. Order effects tested and found no evidence of impact on diagnostic quality, though familiarity with interface increased speed.

02_method.rationale: The study employs three experimental designs, with the first using 'a random sequence of four information treatments' per participant, the second having 'radiologists diagnose patients in each of four information environments,' and the third having 'participants diagnose fifty patients first without and then with AI assistance.' All three designs have the same participants complete multiple conditions, making this a within-subject design despite also incorporating randomization.

per_outcome.operationalization: binary indicator of whether the radiologist's treatment/follow-up recommendation matches the diagnostic standard aggregated from five board-certified radiologists with 10+ years experience
absolute difference between radiologist's reported probability of pathology presence and the diagnostic standard probability from expert panel
time in seconds spent by radiologist on the diagnosis task, measured by experimental platform
absolute difference between human radiologist's reported probability and AI model's reported probability; measures alignment with AI rather than correctness or speed

04_onet.rationale: The participants are professional radiologists performing their core occupational duties: diagnosing pathologies from diagnostic images (chest X-rays), reviewing clinical history and lab results, and making treatment recommendations. The task description explicitly states 'Professional radiologists diagnose the presence of a given pathology in retrospective patient cases' — this is the substantive work of radiologists as an occupation, not a narrow isolated activity. While the study involves retrospective cases rather than real-time clinical work, the radiologists are performing the full scope of diagnostic radiology work across their workday.

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: The study uses a 2×2 factorial design with AI and clinical history (CH) as factors. The AI arm provides AI predictions to radiologists. The comparison is between XO (no AI, no CH) and AI (AI assistance, no CH) to isolate the effect of AI. This is a within-subject design where radiologists diagnose patients under different information conditions.
llm_extracted.notes: Table C.6 (page 55-56) reports treatment effects on Active Time. The AI treatment coefficient is 5.937 (SE=2.347) seconds, meaning AI assistance INCREASES time by ~6 seconds relative to the control mean of 154.317 seconds. This is a within-subject design with 227 radiologists (from summary statistics table C.1). The control mean is reported as 154.317 (SE=4.990). The outcome is in seconds (level, not log-transformed). The positive coefficient indicates AI assistance slightly slows radiologists, contrary to typical productivity gains. The study's main finding is that AI assistance does not improve diagnostic quality on average (section 4.2.2), and this time result shows it also doesn't reduce time spent.
source_quotes: In total, 227 radiologists participated in our experiment.
We find that AI assistance does not improve radiologists' average diagnostic quality, even though its predictions are more accurate than 78% of them.

We also examined the effects of AI assistance and CH on time taken and a proxy for effort (clicks). The expected effects are ambiguous—more information may slow humans but AI assistance could also enable quicker assessments. The results suggests that both AI and CH slow radiologists, but only by 4% in the case of AI

computed.notes: Table C.6 (page 55-56) reports treatment effects on Active Time. The AI treatment coefficient is 5.937 (SE=2.347) seconds, meaning AI assistance INCREASES time by ~6 seconds relative to the control mean of 154.317 seconds. This is a within-subject design with 227 radiologists (from summary statistics table C.1). The control mean is reported as 154.317 (SE=4.990). The outcome is in seconds (level, not log-transformed). The positive coefficient indicates AI assistance slightly slows radiologists, contrary to typical productivity gains. The study's main finding is that AI assistance does not improve diagnostic quality on average (section 4.2.2), and this time result shows it also doesn't reduce time spent. | within-subject mean diff; variance not computable without paired SD

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This is a within-subject design where 227 radiologists diagnosed cases under four conditions: XO (X-ray only), CH (clinical history), AI (AI assistance), and AI+CH (both). The primary comparison is XO vs AI. Table C.6 reports treatment effects using equation (6) with pathology fixed effects. For deviation from diagnostic standard (primary outcome), the AI treatment effect is γ_AI = 0.003 (SE=0.004), p>0.05. The control mean (XO baseline) is 0.226 (SE=0.010). Total observations = 41,920 across 227 radiologists. This is a within-subject design with n=227 radiologists each diagnosing multiple cases. The paper reports treatment effects from a regression model rather than raw means/SDs, but provides sufficient information to extract effect sizes.
llm_extracted.notes: Within-subject design with 227 radiologists. Primary outcome is 'Deviation from Diagnostic Standard' (smaller is better). Table C.6 column (4) reports: Control Mean = 0.226 (SE=0.010), AI treatment effect = 0.003 (SE=0.004), total N=41,920 observations. The AI effect is not statistically significant. M_ai is computed as 0.226 + 0.003 = 0.229. The paper uses two-way clustered standard errors at radiologist and patient level. Sample size n=227 is the number of radiologists (from Table 1 and text). The design involves each radiologist diagnosing multiple cases (92 reads per radiologist on average from Table 1), but the unit of analysis for effect size should be radiologists. The paper does not report SD of differences or paired t-statistics directly, only regression coefficients with clustered SEs. P-value is reported as not significant but exact value not given in table.

Our experimental design compares human and AI performance, quantifies the predictive value of the information that humans hold but AI tools do not—henceforth termed contextual information—and tests whether AI assistance improves human performance.
We report two-way clustered standard errors at the radiologist and patient level
